turns.content: When should I use git rebase instead of merge? I keep hearing conflicting advice.

Use merge when you want to preserve the exact historical shape of how branches diverged and came back together (including explicit merge commits). Use rebase when you want to take a series of commits and “replay” them on top of another base commit to create a cleaner, linear history—at the cost of rewriting commit IDs. A practical rule of thumb: - Rebase your local, unpublished feature work onto an updated base branch to keep your branch up to date and avoid a noisy history. - Merge when integrating completed work into a shared branch, or any time rewriting history would be risky or confusing. The conflicting advice usually comes from mixing two very different contexts: rebasing private work (safe) vs rebasing commits that others might already have based work on (painful).
I think I get the “rewriting history” part, but what exactly is being rewritten? Is Git changing the code or just metadata?

It’s changing commit objects (their IDs) and parent relationships, not the final code content in your working tree—though the working tree will reflect the rebased result. In Git, a commit is a snapshot plus metadata: - Tree (the snapshot of files) - Parent commit(s) - Author/committer info and timestamps - Commit message The commit ID (SHA) is a hash of all that. If you change the parent (which rebase does), you necessarily change the hash, so you get new commit IDs even if the file contents for each commit are identical. Rebase takes commits A–B–C that were originally based on some old base X, and re-creates new commits A'–B'–C' with the same patch content but with a different parent chain on top of new base Y. So history structure and IDs change.
So merge doesn’t do that because it just creates a new commit with two parents?

Exactly. Merge keeps existing commits intact and adds a new merge commit (unless it’s a fast-forward merge). That merge commit has two parents: the tip of the target branch and the tip of the source branch. The original commits remain exactly as they were, so their IDs don’t change. There are a few variants: - Fast-forward merge: if the target branch hasn’t moved, Git just moves the branch pointer forward. No new commit, no merge commit. - True merge commit: if both sides have diverged, Git creates a merge commit. Rebase, by contrast, changes the base of your commits by recreating them.
Can you walk me through a concrete example with commits? Like with letters and branches.

Sure. Say you start with main at commit M1: - main: M1 You create a feature branch and make commits F1 and F2: - main: M1 - feature: M1—F1—F2 Meanwhile main gets a new commit M2: - main: M1—M2 - feature: M1—F1—F2 Now you want feature to incorporate M2. If you merge main into feature: - You run: git checkout feature; git merge main - Git creates a merge commit FM that has parents F2 and M2: Graphically: main:    M1—M2 \ feature: M1—F1—F2—FM History shows the divergence and the merge. If you rebase feature onto main: - You run: git checkout feature; git rebase main - Git takes F1 and F2 and replays them onto M2, creating F1' and F2': main:    M1—M2—F1'—F2' feature:           ^ (points here) Now it’s linear: M1—M2—F1'—F2'
Why do people say rebase makes history “cleaner”? The merge graph seems informative.

“Cleaner” is subjective. Rebase can make it easier to read a log because it becomes a straight line of commits rather than a graph with merges. That can help when you’re scanning `git log` or using tools that display history linearly. But merge commits can be informative too: - They show when and where integration happened. - They preserve the true chronology of parallel development. - They can group a set of commits as “this feature got merged here.” Rebase’s “cleanliness” often means: - No “merge main into feature” commits cluttering history. - Your feature commits appear as if they were built on top of the latest main from the start. - Bisecting can be simpler in linear history (though merge history can also be fine).
I’ve seen lots of “Merge branch 'main' into feature” commits. Are those bad?

Not inherently bad, but they can be noisy if they happen frequently and don’t add meaningful information. Often they’re created when someone regularly merges main into a feature branch just to keep up with changes, producing repeated merge commits that don’t represent a real integration milestone—just synchronization. Rebasing your feature branch onto main avoids those repeated merge commits because you’re replaying your feature commits atop the latest main rather than merging main into feature repeatedly. That said, if you like the explicit record of when syncs occurred, merge commits are not wrong—just potentially verbose.
What about conflicts? Do they differ between merge and rebase?

Conflicts can happen in both, but the experience differs. During a merge: - Git tries to combine the two tips. - If conflicts occur, you resolve them once for the merge commit. During a rebase: - Git applies commits one-by-one. - If conflicts occur, you may resolve conflicts multiple times (once per commit that touches conflicting areas), though Git can sometimes reuse recorded resolutions (via `rerere`) and make it less painful. So rebase can be more granular but sometimes more work if conflicts repeat across commits.
You mentioned “unpublished” commits. How do I know if something is unpublished?

A commit is “published” if other people (or other clones/remotes) might have already fetched it and built work on top of it. In a solo context with one remote, a practical indicator is: have you pushed those commits to a remote that you or others will pull from? If you have pushed a branch and then you rewrite its history (via rebase, amend, filter-repo, etc.), anyone who pulled the old history will have to reconcile their local history with the rewritten one. That’s where pain happens. If the commits exist only in your local repository (not pushed anywhere), rebasing is safe because you’re the only one who knows about those commit IDs.
Suppose I rebased a branch after pushing by accident. What happens, and how do I fix it?

If you rebase and then try to push, Git will reject a non-fast-forward update (by default), because your branch tip changed in a way that discards the remote’s commits (from Git’s perspective). You have a few options: 1) If you truly intend to replace the remote branch history: - Use `git push --force-with-lease origin your-branch` `--force-with-lease` is safer than `--force` because it refuses to overwrite if the remote moved in unexpected ways. 2) If you didn’t intend to rewrite remote history: - You can reset your local branch back to the remote state: - `git fetch origin` - `git reset --hard origin/your-branch` This discards your rebased version locally (unless you recover it via reflog). 3) If you want to keep both histories: - You can create a new branch name for the rebased work and push that, leaving the old one intact. Recovery tips: - `git reflog` can find the pre-rebase commit tips so you can get back to them.
How does reflog help? I’ve never used it.

`git reflog` records where branch references (like `HEAD`, `main`, your feature branch) have pointed over time locally. Even if commits become unreachable via normal branch pointers (like after a reset or rebase), they often remain in the object database for a while, and reflog provides a breadcrumb trail. Example: - You were on `feature` at commit abc123. - You ran rebase and now `feature` points to def456. - `git reflog` might show entries like: - `feature@{1}: rebase: ...` - `feature@{2}: commit: ...` You can then do: - `git reset --hard feature@{2}` (or the specific SHA) to go back. It’s essentially a local safety net.
Okay. But I’m still fuzzy on what “replay commits” means. Is Git applying diffs? Or is it copying snapshots?

Conceptually, rebase is applying the patch (diff) introduced by each commit onto a new base. Internally: - Each commit stores a snapshot (tree), not a diff. - But to “replay” a commit, Git computes the diff between the commit and its parent, then applies that diff onto the new base, creating a new snapshot and a new commit. So yes: it behaves like applying diffs, even though the underlying storage is snapshots. This is why conflicts can occur: applying a patch onto a different base may not apply cleanly.
I’ve also heard about “interactive rebase.” Is that just for squashing?

Squashing is a common use, but interactive rebase (`git rebase -i`) is more general. It lets you rewrite a sequence of commits in many ways: - Reorder commits - Squash/fixup commits (combine them) - Edit commit messages (reword) - Edit commit content (edit) - Drop commits - Split commits (by editing and using `git reset`/`git add -p`) It’s essentially a commit history editor for a linear range of commits.
Can you give me a typical workflow where interactive rebase is helpful, without involving other people?

Sure. Say you’re working on a feature and you made a messy sequence: 1. “WIP: initial attempt” 2. “Fix tests” 3. “Oops, forgot file” 4. “Refactor” 5. “Fix typo” 6. “Final implementation” Before you publish or merge, you might want a more coherent story: - “Implement feature X” - “Add tests for feature X” - “Refactor module Y” You can do: - `git rebase -i <base>` Then mark commits as: - `pick` for the main ones - `squash` or `fixup` for minor fixes into the relevant commit - `reword` to improve messages - reorder if needed Result: a smaller set of meaningful commits.
How do I choose the `<base>` for `git rebase -i`?

You choose the commit just before the range you want to edit. Common ways: - `git rebase -i HEAD~N` to edit the last N commits. - `git rebase -i main` when you’re on a feature branch and want to edit all commits that are on your branch but not on `main`. That uses `main` as the upstream/base. Example: - You’re on `feature`. - `git rebase -i main` opens a list of commits reachable from `feature` but not from `main`.
Wait, `git rebase main` vs `git rebase -i main`—both rebase onto main, but one is interactive?

Correct. Both change the base to `main` (or whatever upstream you specify). The interactive one pauses with a todo list so you can change how commits are replayed. Non-interactive rebase: - Just moves your commits onto the new base, preserving commit order and messages. Interactive: - Lets you modify the sequence and content as you go.
Does rebase always make a branch linear? What about if my branch already has merge commits?

Rebase typically produces a linear sequence of commits on top of the new base, but if your branch contains merge commits, behavior depends on options: - Default `git rebase` flattens history by replaying commits and generally does not preserve merge commits; it can drop the merge structure. - `git rebase --rebase-merges` (or the older `--preserve-merges` in some contexts) attempts to preserve the merge structure by recreating merges during the rebase. So rebase can linearize, but you can ask it to keep merges.
I think I’ve used `--onto` once and got confused. What is `git rebase --onto` for?

`--onto` is for rebasing a subset of commits onto a different base that is not necessarily the upstream of your current branch, or for “moving” a branch off one base onto another. Syntax: - `git rebase --onto <newbase> <upstream> <branch>` Meaning: - Take commits that are in `<branch>` but not in `<upstream>` and replay them onto `<newbase>`. Example scenario: - You branched `feature` off `oldbase`, but you realize it should have been based on `newbase`. - You can do: - `git rebase --onto newbase oldbase feature` This takes commits unique to `feature` since `oldbase` and replays them on `newbase`.
What if I don’t specify `<branch>` in that command?

If you omit `<branch>`, Git assumes the current branch (`HEAD`) is the branch to rebase. So: - `git rebase --onto newbase oldbase` means: rebase the current branch by taking commits since `oldbase` and putting them on `newbase`.
Is there a way to see what commits will be rebased before doing it?

Yes. You can inspect the commit range with `git log` or `git rev-list`. If you plan `git rebase main` while on `feature`, the commits to be replayed are roughly: - `main..feature` (commits reachable from feature but not from main) You can preview: - `git log --oneline main..feature` or for a graph: - `git log --oneline --graph --decorate --boundary main...feature` The triple-dot `...` shows symmetric difference; double-dot `..` shows commits in the second not in the first.
The `..` vs `...` thing always trips me up. Can you explain them in this context?

Sure. - `A..B` means: commits reachable from B that are not reachable from A. So `main..feature` = “what’s on feature that main doesn’t have.” - `A...B` means: commits reachable from either A or B but not both (symmetric difference). It’s useful for seeing divergence on both sides. In a typical rebase scenario, you care about `upstream..branch` to see what will be replayed.
If rebase rewrites commit IDs, what does that do to tags or references?

Tags and references point to specific commits. If you rebase and thereby create new commits, any tag that pointed to an old commit will still point to that old commit. This can matter if: - You tagged a commit that later gets rebased away; the tag still points to the pre-rebase commit. - You rely on commit IDs in documentation or scripts; they won’t match after rebasing. Branches are just pointers too; rebasing moves the branch pointer to the new tip (and the old commits become unreferenced unless something else points to them).
How do I decide between “merge main into feature” and “rebase feature onto main” while I’m actively working?

If your goal is to keep your feature branch up to date and reduce future integration conflicts, rebasing is often nicer for a linear story and avoids repeated merge commits. A common personal workflow: - Periodically: `git fetch origin` - Then: `git rebase origin/main` (while on feature) - Resolve conflicts as needed. - Continue working. If you prefer never rewriting your branch history (even locally), you can merge main into feature instead: - `git merge origin/main` But that can add sync merges. The trade-off is mostly about history shape and conflict resolution style, not correctness.
Is there any difference in the final code that ends up on main if I rebase vs merge?

Usually, the final file content on main can be identical, but the commit graph differs. However, there are subtle differences: - With merge, you may get a merge commit that combines two lines of development. The merge commit itself has a snapshot that could differ slightly if conflict resolutions are done differently. - With rebase, the conflict resolutions happen while replaying commits; the resulting commits include those resolutions in the rebased commits. So the end state can be the same, but which commits contain which changes differs, and sometimes that matters for bisecting or blame.
Speaking of blame, people say rebase makes blame easier. True?

It can, depending on how you structure commits. Pros: - If you squash “fixup” commits into the commit that introduced the change, `git blame` points to a meaningful commit rather than a later “fix typo” commit. - Linear history can make it easier to track when something was introduced. Cons: - If you heavily rewrite history, the commit IDs referenced in bug reports or past notes become invalid. - If you squash too much, you might lose granular history that helps debugging. So “easier blame” is more about curating commit structure than rebase itself.
What exactly is a “fast-forward” merge, and how does that relate to rebase?

A fast-forward merge happens when the branch you’re merging into hasn’t diverged from the branch you’re merging from. Example: - main: M1 - feature: M1—F1—F2 If you merge feature into main and main is still at M1: - Git can just move main pointer to F2. - No merge commit needed. That’s a fast-forward. Relation to rebase: - Rebasing a branch onto main can make later integration a fast-forward if main hasn’t moved since you rebased and you merge in the right direction. - But fast-forward depends on graph shape at merge time.
I’ve seen `--no-ff` merges. Why would I force a merge commit?

`--no-ff` forces a merge commit even when a fast-forward is possible. People use it to preserve a visible “merge boundary” that groups a feature’s work. Without `--no-ff`, merging a feature branch that’s strictly ahead would just move the pointer, and the fact that it was a separate branch is not visible in history. With `--no-ff`, you get a merge commit that can serve as a marker: “feature X integrated here.” This can help in navigation and in reverting a whole feature (sometimes) by reverting the merge commit.
Wait, reverting a merge commit is special, right?

Yes. Reverting a merge commit requires telling Git which parent to consider the “mainline,” because a merge commit has multiple parents. Command: - `git revert -m 1 <merge-commit-sha>` where `-m 1` typically means “keep the first parent’s content and undo the changes from the other parent.” This can be convenient if a feature was merged via a merge commit and you want to back it out as a unit. If the feature was fast-forwarded or rebased/squashed into main without a merge commit, reverting “the feature” may involve reverting multiple commits instead.
So if I rebase and then squash, I lose the ability to revert the feature easily?

You might lose the single-merge-commit revert shortcut, but you can still revert: - If it’s squashed into one commit, reverting is easy: revert that single commit. - If it’s rebased but not squashed and integrated as a fast-forward, you can revert a range of commits (or revert each commit). The “easy revert” aspect depends on how the feature appears in main: merge commit vs single squashed commit vs multiple commits.
I’m also confused about `git pull --rebase`. What does it do exactly?

`git pull` is roughly: 1) `git fetch` 2) then integrate fetched changes into your current branch. By default, integration is a merge: - `git pull` → fetch + merge With `--rebase`, integration is a rebase: - `git pull --rebase` → fetch + rebase your local commits on top of the updated upstream tracking branch. Example: - You have local commits not pushed yet. - Upstream branch advanced. - `git pull --rebase` will replay your local commits on top of the newly fetched upstream commits, resulting in a linear history and avoiding a merge commit from the pull.
Does `pull --rebase` rewrite history too? That sounds dangerous.

It rewrites your local commits (the ones you have locally that are not in the upstream branch). It does not rewrite the remote’s history; it’s moving your local work on top of it. It’s generally safe if: - Your local commits are not yet pushed. - Or, if they are pushed, you understand the implications and are prepared to force-push (which is usually not what you want from a simple pull). So it’s “dangerous” only if you use it on a branch where your local commits are already published and shared.
I sometimes get into a state where `git pull --rebase` stops with conflicts, and I don’t know what to do.

When a rebase stops due to conflicts, you’re in the middle of a rebase. The steps are: 1) Check status: - `git status` It will say you’re rebasing and which commit is being applied. 2) Resolve conflicts in files: - Edit files, remove conflict markers. - Stage resolutions: `git add <file>` 3) Continue: - `git rebase --continue` If you want to abort the entire rebase and go back to where you were: - `git rebase --abort` If you want to skip the problematic commit (rarely ideal): - `git rebase --skip` That’s the main loop: resolve → add → continue.
How is that different from merge conflict resolution?

For a merge conflict: - You resolve conflicts once, stage, then `git commit` (or Git may auto-create the commit message for the merge commit). - Or you can abort merge with `git merge --abort`. For a rebase conflict: - You resolve per commit, stage, then `git rebase --continue`. - Abort with `git rebase --abort`. The mechanics of resolving conflict markers in files is the same; the control commands differ.
I’ve seen `git rerere`. Is that related?

Yes. `rerere` stands for “reuse recorded resolution.” If enabled, Git records how you resolved a conflict and can automatically apply the same resolution if the same conflict appears again in future merges/rebases. It’s helpful when: - You frequently rebase a long-lived branch and hit the same conflicts repeatedly. - You do repeated merges between branches. Enable it: - `git config --global rerere.enabled true` Then Git will store conflict resolution data in `.git/rr-cache`.
Nice. Another confusion: is rebase basically cherry-pick of a range?

Conceptually, yes. Rebase is similar to doing a series of cherry-picks. If you run: - `git rebase newbase` Git is essentially: - find the commits on your branch not in newbase - for each commit, cherry-pick it onto newbase - move your branch pointer That’s why conflicts and replay mechanics feel like cherry-pick. But rebase also has additional behavior around upstream tracking, interactive editing, and options like preserving merges.
If that’s the case, why not just cherry-pick?

Cherry-pick is great when you want to apply specific commits from one branch onto another, not necessarily preserving the branch’s identity. Rebase is more convenient when you want to move an entire branch’s line of development to a new base—especially when you want to keep the same branch name and rewrite the sequence as a unit. Also: - Rebase can automatically determine the correct range of commits based on upstream. - Interactive rebase provides a rich interface for rewriting sequences. - Cherry-pick doesn’t automatically “rebase” the branch pointer; you’re applying commits onto your current branch.
When I do `git rebase main`, does it automatically know the fork point?

It uses the merge-base (common ancestor) between your current branch and `main` to determine which commits are unique to your branch and should be replayed. However, there are subtle cases where the “fork point” is not what you expect (e.g., if your branch was created, then main was merged into it, etc.). There’s an option: - `git rebase --fork-point` This can help when rebasing a topic branch that was based on a remote-tracking branch and there were updates that make the merge-base calculation include commits that shouldn’t be considered part of your topic branch. In many everyday cases, default behavior is fine.
Can you explain a case where `--fork-point` matters?

One classic case: - You branch off `origin/main` at some point. - Later, `origin/main` gets fast-forwarded (normal). - You rebase your branch onto `origin/main`. If you previously merged `origin/main` into your branch (creating merge commits), the merge-base might move in a way that makes Git think some commits are part of your branch’s unique history when they aren’t. `--fork-point` tries to find where your branch originally forked from the upstream branch by using reflog information of the remote-tracking branch. It’s a bit niche, but it can prevent accidentally replaying commits that already exist upstream.
What about `git pull --rebase`—does it use fork-point?

By default, `git pull --rebase` may use `--fork-point` depending on your Git version and configuration. There is a config: - `pull.rebase` (true/false/merges/interactive) - `rebase.forkPoint` (true/false) You can set: - `git config --global pull.rebase true` - `git config --global rebase.forkPoint true` But behavior can vary; checking `git help pull` and `git help rebase` for your version is useful.
You mentioned `pull.rebase` can be “merges” or “interactive”? What does that mean?

`pull.rebase` supports values like: - `false`: default merge behavior. - `true`: rebase local commits on top of fetched branch. - `merges`: rebase but try to preserve merge commits (like `--rebase-merges`). - `interactive`: run an interactive rebase during pull (less common; it can open an editor). So you can tailor how pulls integrate changes.
Now I’m thinking about “squash merge” on hosting sites. Is that the same as rebase?

Squash merge is a type of integration that takes all commits from a branch/PR and combines them into a single new commit on the target branch. It’s not exactly rebase, but it has similar effects: - It creates new commit(s) with new IDs. - It can produce a linear history. - It discards the original individual commits from the target branch history (though they still exist on the feature branch if it remains). Compared to rebase: - Rebase keeps multiple commits (unless you squash). - Squash merge always creates one commit (for that integration), usually with a message summarizing the changes.
If squash merge creates a new commit, doesn’t that also “rewrite history”? Why is it considered okay?

It creates new history on the target branch, but it doesn’t rewrite existing commits on the target branch. That’s the key difference. Rewriting history means changing commits that are already part of a branch’s existing published history. Squash merge adds a new commit; it doesn’t alter old ones. It does mean the individual feature branch commits aren’t part of the target branch history, but that’s not “rewriting” the target’s past; it’s choosing a different representation of the integration.
Okay. So if I want a linear history on main, I can either rebase-and-merge or squash merge?

Yes, broadly: - Rebase-and-merge: replay feature commits onto main and then fast-forward main (or merge fast-forward). Main gets each feature commit individually (with new IDs if rebased). - Squash merge: main gets one commit representing the net change. Both yield linear history; they just differ in how much commit granularity is kept.
Which is better for debugging with `git bisect`?

Depends. - If you keep multiple meaningful commits (rebase-and-merge without squashing), bisect can pinpoint the exact commit that introduced a bug within the feature. - If you squash everything into one commit, bisect can only identify that squashed commit, not the internal steps. However, if your intermediate commits are messy or broken (e.g., tests failing mid-way), squashing can make bisect more pleasant because every commit on main is “good” (builds/tests pass). You can also achieve that with rebase by squashing or editing commits to ensure each is coherent. So “better” depends on whether your individual commits are reliable and meaningful.
Is it a good practice to ensure every commit passes tests?

It’s a nice goal, especially for main branch history, because it makes `git bisect` and historical checkouts more reliable. But it’s not always practical during local work. A common approach: - Allow messy commits locally. - Before publishing/integrating, use interactive rebase to squash/fixup and reorder so the final commit sequence is logical and each commit is in a good state. That balances experimentation with maintainable history.
What about `git commit --amend`? Is that also rewriting history?

Yes. `--amend` replaces the most recent commit with a new one: - It creates a new commit object with a new ID (even if only the message changed). - The old commit becomes dangling (unless referenced elsewhere). Amending is safe for local, unpublished commits. If you amend a commit that’s already pushed and then push, you’ll need to force push, and anyone who fetched the old commit will have divergence.
Sometimes I do a rebase and end up with duplicate commits or weird history. How does that happen?

Common causes: 1) Rebasing a branch that already contains merged commits from the target branch, without preserving merges, can cause Git to replay commits in a way that appears duplicative. 2) Cherry-picking commits from another branch and later rebasing can produce duplicates if Git can’t detect they’re equivalent. 3) The same change can appear with different commit IDs; Git identifies commits by hash, not by patch equivalence (though it can sometimes detect “already applied” patches with options). There are options to help: - `git rebase --onto ...` with correct upstream selection prevents replaying too much. - `git cherry` can show which commits are equivalent by patch-id. - `git rebase --rebase-merges` can preserve structure and avoid flattening merges in confusing ways.
What is “patch-id”?

Patch-id is a way Git computes an approximate identity for a commit based on the diff content, ignoring things like commit message and some context. Two commits with the same patch-id are likely to represent the same change, even if they have different commit IDs (e.g., due to cherry-pick or rebase). `git cherry` uses patch-id to compare branches and indicate which commits are unique or already applied in some form.
So can rebase detect that a commit was already applied and skip it?

Sometimes. There’s an option: - `git rebase --reapply-cherry-picks` (controls behavior with cherry-picked commits) And rebase has logic to drop commits that are already upstream when it can detect them (based on patch-id heuristics). But it’s not perfect, especially with conflicts, context changes, or non-trivial diffs.
How do I inspect the merge base and understand what Git thinks is common?

Use: - `git merge-base <branch1> <branch2>` It outputs the SHA of the best common ancestor. You can also visualize: - `git log --graph --oneline --decorate --all` and look for where the branches diverge. Or: - `git show $(git merge-base main feature)` to see that base commit. Understanding merge-base helps predict what rebase will replay.
If merge preserves history, why would anyone prefer rebase at all?

Because merge can create a lot of non-linear history, especially if you frequently synchronize branches. Rebase offers: - A linear narrative: your feature commits appear as a clean sequence atop the base. - Fewer merge commits from syncing. - Easier to reason about “what did I do?” on a feature branch via `git log main..feature`. Also, interactive rebase is a powerful tool for cleaning up local history before publishing. So rebase isn’t about correctness; it’s about shaping history for readability and maintainability.
I’ve heard “never rebase main.” Is that a hard rule?

It’s a practical rule: don’t rebase a branch that’s serving as a stable base and is widely referenced, because rebasing rewrites history and forces everyone (or every clone) to reconcile. Even without mentioning other people, it can break: - CI references to specific SHAs - tags pointing into that history - downstream branches based on those SHAs - any automation or documentation that references commit IDs So “never rebase main” is really “never rewrite published shared history.” Main is typically that.
What about rebasing a long-lived branch to keep it updated? Is that okay?

It depends on whether that branch is treated as a stable reference and whether others/systems rely on its commit IDs. Technically it’s possible, but long-lived branches are more likely to be “published” in the sense that other things depend on them. Rewriting them frequently can be disruptive. If you want to keep it updated without rewriting, merging main into it periodically is safer.
Let’s talk about `git rebase --autosquash`. How does that work?

`--autosquash` works with commits whose messages start with: - `fixup! <target subject>` - `squash! <target subject>` If you create a fixup commit: - `git commit --fixup <commit>` Git makes a new commit with message `fixup! ...` referencing the target commit’s subject. Then when you do: - `git rebase -i --autosquash <base>` Git automatically reorders the fixup/squash commits to be right after their targets and marks them as `fixup` or `squash` in the interactive todo list. This makes it easy to create small follow-up commits and later automatically fold them into the right place.
So the workflow is: make fixup commits as I go, then autosquash later?

Exactly. You can keep momentum while working: - Make a commit even if it’s a small correction. - Use `--fixup` so it’s tagged to a target commit. Then later: - interactive rebase with autosquash - Git lines them up automatically - you end up with a clean set of commits.
How do I pick the commit for `--fixup`? By SHA?

You can specify: - A SHA (full or abbreviated) - A ref like `HEAD~3` - Or use interactive tools: - `git log --oneline` to find it - Some setups have `git commit --fixup :/searchstring` to search commit messages (the `:/` syntax searches the commit message history). Example: - `git commit --fixup HEAD~2`
Nice. Another thing: sometimes I see “rebase succeeded but detached HEAD” or I end up detached. Why?

Detached HEAD means `HEAD` points directly to a commit rather than to a branch ref. This can happen if you checked out a commit SHA or a tag, or during certain operations. Rebase itself usually operates on a branch. But you might end up detached if: - You started rebase from a detached HEAD state. - Or you checked out `origin/main` (a remote-tracking branch) directly and then rebased; remote-tracking refs aren’t local branches. Fix: - Create or switch to a local branch: - `git switch -c mybranch` (from detached state) or - `git switch mybranch` Then rebase from that branch.
What’s the difference between `origin/main` and `main` in this context?

`origin/main` is a remote-tracking branch: your local record of what the remote’s `main` looked like the last time you fetched. `main` is your local branch. They often point to the same commit, but not always: - After `git fetch`, `origin/main` updates. - Your `main` updates when you merge/rebase/pull. You typically rebase your feature branch onto `origin/main` after fetching to ensure you’re basing on the latest fetched remote state: - `git fetch origin` - `git rebase origin/main`
If I do `git rebase main` without fetching, is that bad?

Not bad, just potentially rebasing onto an outdated local `main` if it’s behind the remote. If your local main is current, it’s fine. If not, you might still have to integrate more changes later. A safe pattern: - `git fetch` - `git rebase origin/main` (or update your local main first)
How do I update local main safely?

Common sequence: - `git switch main` - `git fetch origin` - `git merge --ff-only origin/main` (or `git pull --ff-only`) `--ff-only` ensures you only fast-forward, avoiding accidental merge commits on main. If it can’t fast-forward, it fails, prompting you to inspect what’s going on. Then switch back to your feature branch and rebase onto the updated main.
So `--ff-only` is like a safety check.
Exactly. It prevents creating a merge commit on a branch where you expect linear fast-forward updates.
If I rebase my feature branch onto main, do I need to force push?

If your feature branch has been pushed and you rebase it (changing commit IDs), then yes, pushing the rebased branch will require a force push because the remote branch history and your local branch history diverge. If it hasn’t been pushed, a normal push is fine. If you do force push, prefer: - `git push --force-with-lease` It’s a safer force that checks the remote ref hasn’t changed unexpectedly.
I’m trying to internalize this. Can we do another example with actual commands?

Sure. Example: you have a feature branch and want to keep it current via rebase. 1) Start: - `git switch -c feature` - make commits: `git commit ...` a few times 2) Meanwhile, main advances on the remote. You want to update: - `git fetch origin` 3) Rebase your feature onto updated main: - `git switch feature` - `git rebase origin/main` 4) Resolve conflicts if any: - edit files - `git add ...` - `git rebase --continue` 5) If feature is already on the remote and you rebased: - `git push --force-with-lease origin feature` If feature is not on remote yet: - `git push -u origin feature`
What about merging instead? Commands?

Merge-based update: 1) `git fetch origin` 2) `git switch feature` 3) `git merge origin/main` If conflicts: - resolve - `git add ...` - `git commit` (Git prepares a merge message) Then push normally: - `git push origin feature` No force push because merge doesn’t rewrite existing commits.
Is one approach more likely to cause conflicts later?

Not necessarily. Both integrate changes; both can surface conflicts. But the timing differs: - Rebase surfaces conflicts at rebase time, potentially multiple times across commits. - Merge surfaces conflicts at merge time, once. If you rebase frequently, you may resolve smaller conflicts earlier and keep your branch closer to main, which can make the final integration smoother. If you merge main into your branch frequently, you might avoid repeated conflict resolution per commit (since it’s one merge), but you’ll accumulate merge commits.
I’ve heard “rebase is dangerous because it changes history,” but then everyone recommends `pull --rebase`. Is the danger overstated?

The danger is contextual. Rebase is dangerous when you rewrite commits that are already used as a base for other work or referenced externally. But rebasing local, private commits is a very normal and safe thing. `pull --rebase` is recommended in contexts where: - You have local commits not yet pushed. - You want to avoid merge commits from pulling. - You’re comfortable resolving rebase conflicts. So it’s not that rebase is inherently dangerous; it’s that rewriting published history has consequences.
What does “published” mean if I’m the only one using the remote?

Even if you’re the only human, “published” can still mean “referenced by something else”: - automated builds referencing SHAs - tags/releases - other branches you created from those commits - scripts or notes referencing commit IDs If none of that applies and you’re okay rewriting your own remote branch history, then force-pushing rebased branches can be fine. The main point is: once a commit ID is relied upon, rewriting it breaks that reference.
Makes sense. Another question: does rebase change authorship info?

It can. Each commit has an author and a committer: - Author: who originally wrote the change. - Committer: who last applied it to the repository history. When you rebase, you’re creating new commits, so you become the committer of the new commits (with a new committer timestamp). The author typically stays the same as the original commit’s author, unless you change it. This is why rebased commits often show updated dates (committer date), while author dates may remain older.
Can I preserve the original commit dates?

You can control dates, but by default: - author date stays - committer date updates There are options like `--committer-date-is-author-date` or environment variables to set dates, but it’s uncommon unless you have a specific need. Most of the time, seeing a newer committer date on rebased commits is expected.
How do merge commits affect author/committer?

Merge commits have their own author/committer info (usually the person who performed the merge). The commits being merged retain their original metadata. So merge adds a new commit with its own metadata, while rebase recreates commits (new committer info).
I’m curious about the underlying data model. You said commits have parents. How do merges store two parents?

A normal commit has one parent (except the initial commit which has none). A merge commit has multiple parents—typically two, but it can have more in an “octopus merge.” The commit object stores: - Parent SHA(s) - Tree SHA - metadata When Git computes the merge result, it creates a new tree representing the merged snapshot and writes a commit with two parent pointers. This is why the history becomes a directed acyclic graph (DAG), not a simple list.
And rebase just changes the parent pointer of each commit by recreating it?
Exactly. Since you can’t “edit” an existing commit object (it’s content-addressed), Git creates new commits with the desired parent pointers.
That helps. What about “octopus merges”? Do they matter here?

Mostly not for everyday rebase vs merge decisions, but conceptually: - Merge can have more than two parents (merging multiple branches at once). - Rebase doesn’t naturally create multi-parent commits unless you use `--rebase-merges` to preserve merge commits, in which case it can recreate merge structure but typically still as a sequence of merges. Octopus merges are mainly used when combining multiple heads that don’t conflict.
If rebase creates new commits, does it also duplicate file blobs and use more disk?

Often not significantly, because Git deduplicates objects by content (hash). If the file contents (blobs) and trees are the same, Git reuses them; only commit objects are new. Rebased commits create new commit objects, and potentially new tree objects if the snapshots differ due to conflict resolutions or changed parent context. But blobs are reused if identical. So disk impact is usually small.
Is there any performance difference between merge and rebase?

For typical sizes, not much. For very large repositories or huge ranges of commits: - Rebase has to replay each commit, which can be more work than a single merge. - Interactive rebase can be heavier. - Merge is a single operation that creates one commit (or fast-forward). But in practice, the difference is usually dominated by conflict resolution time, not CPU.
I want a mental model. Is merge like “combine two histories,” and rebase like “move my history”?

That’s a good model: - Merge: combine two lines of history; preserve both; add a merge commit to join them. - Rebase: relocate your line of commits to a new base; rewrite those commits as if they started from the new base.
How do I explain this to myself when looking at a graph?

When you see a graph: - Merge: you’ll see a branch line diverge and then a merge node where lines converge. - Rebase: you’ll see your commits sitting directly on top of the new base, and the old line (if still visible) becomes orphaned. In tools, after a rebase, the old commits might disappear from normal branch history views unless referenced, because the branch pointer moved.
What about `git log --first-parent`? I’ve seen that mentioned with merge commits.

`--first-parent` makes `git log` follow only the first parent of merge commits. This is useful when you have merge commits and you want to see the mainline history without diving into merged branch details. If you often merge feature branches into main with merge commits, `git log --first-parent` gives a clean view of integration points. This is one reason some people like merge commits: you can keep detail in the graph but still view a simplified linear “mainline” with `--first-parent`.
That’s interesting. So merge history isn’t necessarily messy if you know how to view it.
Right. Merge history can be navigated with the right log options and graph viewers. The “messy” perception often comes from repeated sync merges or tools that display history poorly.
So if I want clean history, I can choose either: rebase to linearize, or merge but view with first-parent.

Exactly. Different philosophies: - Linear history: rebase/squash to keep main as a straight line. - True history with merges: keep merge commits; use first-parent to view mainline; keep full graph for deeper inspection. Both can be reasonable.
Let’s talk about “conflict markers.” When I rebase, I sometimes get conflicts that I don’t get when merging. Is that possible?

Yes, because the conflict context can differ. When merging: - Git combines changes from two tips, using a 3-way merge with a common ancestor. When rebasing: - Git applies each commit’s patch onto the new base. The patch may apply differently, and conflicts can arise per commit. Sometimes a merge can resolve cleanly because the combined changes are compatible in the final state, but individual commits during rebase may conflict because intermediate states differ. Conversely, rebase can be clean while merge conflicts, depending on what changed.
So rebase conflicts might be more “granular” but also more frequent.
Exactly. It’s a trade-off: more steps, potentially more conflict events, but each event might be smaller and tied to a specific commit.
Are there strategies to reduce rebase conflicts?

A few: - Rebase more frequently, so the delta between your branch and base is smaller. - Keep commits focused and small; avoid huge commits touching many areas. - Use `git rerere` to reuse conflict resolutions. - When conflicts keep repeating, consider squashing related commits so you resolve once (though that’s a history choice). - Use `git add -p` to craft commits that isolate changes.
What does `git add -p` do?

`git add -p` (patch mode) lets you interactively stage parts of files (hunks) rather than staging the entire file. This helps you: - Create smaller, more focused commits. - Separate unrelated changes. - Reduce conflict surface area. It’s very useful in combination with rebase workflows because clean commits are easier to rebase and understand.
If I have a messy working tree and want to split changes into commits, can interactive rebase help?

Interactive rebase is for rewriting commits that already exist. For splitting uncommitted working tree changes, you’d use staging tools: - `git add -p` to stage parts - `git commit` to commit staged parts Repeat. To split an existing commit, interactive rebase can help: - `git rebase -i <base>` - mark the commit as `edit` - when rebase stops, you can: - `git reset HEAD^` (or `git reset --mixed HEAD^`) to uncommit but keep changes - then stage/commit pieces - `git rebase --continue` That’s how you split a commit.
That’s powerful. But also scary.

It can be intimidating, but it’s deterministic if you know the steps. And reflog provides recovery if you get lost. A safety trick: before major history edits, create a backup branch: - `git branch backup-feature` Then even if you rewrite `feature`, you can recover from `backup-feature`.
Good idea. So I can always branch off before rebasing.
Yes, it’s a simple and effective safety net.
Another question: how does `git merge --squash` compare to rebase-squash?

`git merge --squash <branch>` takes the changes introduced by `<branch>` and stages them as a single combined change on your current branch, but it does not create a merge commit and does not record branch ancestry. You then run `git commit` to create one commit. Compared to rebase + squash: - Both can result in a single commit on the target branch. - `merge --squash` doesn’t preserve any relationship in history that the commits came from that branch. - Rebase + squash (or squash merge in hosting tools) similarly results in a single commit on main, but the mechanics differ. If you care about preserving “this came from branch X” via ancestry, squash approaches lose that information.
So if I later want to see what commits were in the feature branch, squash merge loses it?

On the target branch, yes—you only see the squashed commit. The original commits still exist on the feature branch if it still exists. If the feature branch is deleted and no other refs point to those commits, they may eventually be garbage collected. So if you squash and delete the source branch, you might lose the detailed commit history unless it’s kept elsewhere.
That’s a trade-off.
Exactly: clean, minimal history vs retaining detailed incremental commits.
Let’s switch to how to configure Git for a preferred workflow. What settings matter for rebase vs merge?

Useful configs: - `pull.rebase`: - `git config --global pull.rebase true` (or false) - `rebase.autoStash`: - `git config --global rebase.autoStash true` This stashes local uncommitted changes automatically before rebase and re-applies them after, to avoid “cannot rebase: you have unstaged changes” errors. - `branch.<name>.rebase`: Set per-branch rebase behavior for pull. - `merge.ff`: - `git config --global merge.ff only` to enforce fast-forward-only merges by default (be careful; it can block merges that need a merge commit). - `fetch.prune`: - `git config --global fetch.prune true` to clean up deleted remote branches.
That `rebase.autoStash` sounds useful. Any downsides?

It’s convenient, but be aware: - It creates an automatic stash entry and pops it later. If conflicts occur when reapplying the stash, you’ll need to resolve them. - If something goes wrong, you may need to manually apply or drop the stash. Usually it’s fine, but it’s good to know it’s happening. You can see stashes with `git stash list`.
If I’m in the middle of a rebase and I realize I need to do something else, can I pause?

Yes. While a rebase is stopped (e.g., due to conflicts or because you used `edit`), your repo is in a special state. You can: - Fix things and continue. - Or abort. If you need to temporarily switch context, it’s best to either: - Finish the current conflict resolution and continue to a stable state, or - Abort the rebase, do your other tasks, then retry. Switching branches mid-rebase is possible but can get confusing; Git may prevent it or you might end up with a messy working tree.
What is the `.git/rebase-apply` directory I sometimes see mentioned?

During a rebase, Git stores state in `.git/rebase-apply` (or `.git/rebase-merge` for interactive/merge-preserving rebases). These directories track: - which commits are being applied - the todo list (for interactive) - current patch, etc. You generally don’t edit these manually; they’re implementation details. But knowing they exist helps explain why Git knows you’re “in the middle of a rebase.”
If something gets stuck, is it safe to delete those directories?

It’s usually better to use the proper commands: - `git rebase --abort` - `git rebase --continue` - `git rebase --quit` Manually deleting rebase state directories can leave your repo in a confusing state. If commands fail and you’re stuck, you can sometimes recover with reflog and reset, but it’s a last resort.
What does `git rebase --quit` do?

`--quit` stops the rebase and cleans up the rebase state, but it does not reset your branch back to the original state like `--abort` does. So: - `--abort`: return to pre-rebase state (as if rebase never started). - `--quit`: stop the rebase and leave whatever changes are currently in your working tree/index, but remove the “rebase in progress” metadata. `--quit` is less commonly used, but it’s there.
You mentioned `--rebase-merges`. How does interactive rebase work with merges?

`git rebase -i --rebase-merges <upstream>` will open a more complex todo list that includes commands to recreate merges. It’s more advanced because it’s not just a list of linear commits; it includes labels and merge instructions. It’s useful when you have a branch with merges you want to preserve but still want to edit history (like reordering, squashing within sub-branches, etc.). But it’s more complex and easier to get wrong.
So for most cases, I can ignore that and keep branches mostly linear.
Yes. If you keep your topic branches linear (avoid merging into them, and instead rebase them onto the base), then standard rebase is straightforward.
Another question: If I merge main into feature occasionally, then later I want to rebase feature onto main, is that a bad idea?

It can be confusing because your feature branch now contains merge commits from main, and rebasing can flatten or rearrange things. It’s not impossible, but you might see: - duplicate-looking commits - surprising conflict points - a much larger rebase than expected If you want to rebase-based workflow, it’s usually cleaner to avoid merging main into feature in the first place and instead rebase feature onto main regularly. If you already have merge commits, consider: - continuing with merge-based approach, or - using `git rebase --rebase-merges` if preserving merges matters, or - creating a new clean branch and cherry-picking your feature commits onto it.
Cherry-picking onto a new branch sounds like a reset.

It is a kind of “manual rebase.” Sometimes it’s simpler: - Create new branch from main: - `git switch -c feature2 main` - Cherry-pick the meaningful commits from feature: - `git cherry-pick <sha1> <sha2> ...` Or cherry-pick a range if linear: - `git cherry-pick A..B` It avoids dealing with merges in the old branch, at the cost of manual selection.
How do I cherry-pick a range correctly?

If you have a linear sequence and want to cherry-pick commits after A up to and including B: - `git cherry-pick A..B` This means “commits reachable from B but not from A” (so it excludes A itself). If you want to include A too, you can use: - `git cherry-pick A^..B` (parent of A to B) Be careful with ranges on non-linear history; it can pick more than you expect.
Coming back to merge vs rebase: is there any case where merge is clearly better for a feature branch?

Merge is clearly better when you do not want to rewrite history and you want to preserve the exact integration points. Also: - If your branch includes merges intentionally (like you’re combining multiple lines of work) and you want that structure preserved, merging is natural. - If you expect frequent pulling/pushing of the same branch from multiple places, rewriting its history with rebase can cause constant divergence. Even without mentioning team context, the point is: if multiple clones or processes are consuming that branch as a base, merge avoids rewriting and is safer.
I think I’m leaning toward rebasing my feature branches locally, and merging into main with fast-forward or squash. But I don’t know how to ensure I don’t accidentally rebase something I shouldn’t.

A few guardrails: - Only rebase branches you consider “local topic branches.” - Avoid rebasing branches you treat as stable bases or that you’ve tagged/released. - If you do push a topic branch and rebase it later, use `--force-with-lease` and be aware you’re rewriting that branch’s remote history. - Before rebasing, create a backup branch: - `git branch backup/<branchname>` - Use `git status` and `git branch -vv` to see tracking info and whether a branch is ahead/behind its upstream.
What does `git branch -vv` show?

It shows local branches with: - the upstream tracking branch (if set) - ahead/behind counts - last commit message Example output: - `feature  abc123 [origin/feature: ahead 2] ...` This helps you see whether you have local commits not pushed, or whether the remote has commits you haven’t integrated.
If I’m “behind,” and I rebase, will it incorporate remote changes?

If you rebase onto the upstream branch that contains those remote changes, yes. Example: - Your branch tracks `origin/feature`. - Remote has new commits; you’re behind. If you do: - `git fetch` - `git rebase origin/feature` You’re rebasing your local commits (if any) on top of the updated remote branch tip. But if you have no local commits and you’re simply behind, you might just fast-forward instead (merge ff-only).
That sounds like a weird case: rebasing onto the same branch name.

It can happen when you have local commits plus remote commits both on the same branch name (divergence). Rebasing onto the remote-tracking branch is a way to linearize: put your local commits on top of the updated remote state. But again, if you then push, you might be able to push without force if it becomes fast-forward, or you might still need force if the remote also had divergent history. Usually, if the remote advanced and you’re just replaying your local commits atop it, the push is fast-forward.
What about “merge commit vs rebase” in terms of readability in code review? I know you said no work environment stuff, but purely in terms of reading diffs.

Purely in terms of reading diffs and commit series: - A well-curated rebase (small logical commits, clean messages) makes it easier to review commit-by-commit because each commit is meaningful. - A merge-based approach can also be reviewable if you avoid noisy sync merges; merge commits themselves typically don’t add diff content beyond conflict resolutions, but they add graph complexity. Squash merges make review history minimal: one commit with the final diff. That can be easier if you don’t care about intermediate steps, harder if you want to understand incremental reasoning.
Are there any commands to compare what merge would do vs what rebase would do?

You can simulate or inspect without committing: - For merge: - `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` (does the merge and leaves changes staged/working tree for inspection) - Then abort with `git merge --abort` if you don’t want it. - For rebase: - Harder to “preview” because it rewrites commits, but you can: - Create a temporary branch and rebase it - Or use `git rebase --onto` on a temporary branch - Or inspect commit ranges and diffs: - `git log main..feature` - `git diff main...feature` (diff of tips relative to merge-base) Also: - `git range-diff <oldbase>..<oldtip> <newbase>..<newtip>` is great for comparing two versions of a patch series (e.g., before and after rebase).
`git range-diff`—I’ve never used that. What does it show?

It compares two commit ranges as sequences of patches, showing which commits correspond, which changed, which were added/dropped, etc. It’s like a “diff of patch series.” Example usage: - Before rebasing, note old tip `old`. - After rebasing, new tip `new`. Then: - `git range-diff main..old main..new` This helps confirm your rebased series still contains the same logical changes, just with different commit IDs, and highlights any accidental differences.
That seems super useful for not messing up during rebase.
Exactly. It’s a good verification tool.
I want to ask about “merge strategies” like `-s ours` or `-X theirs`. Are those relevant to rebase?

They’re mostly merge-related, but there are parallels. For merge: - `-s ours` means “keep our side’s content, but record a merge.” - `-X theirs` is an option to favor the other side in conflict resolution (for conflicting hunks). For rebase: - You can pass merge strategy options to rebase too, because rebase uses merge machinery when applying patches (especially for conflict resolution). Example: - `git rebase -X theirs <upstream>` But be careful: “theirs” in rebase context can be counterintuitive depending on which side is considered “theirs” (the commit being applied vs the base). It’s easy to misuse. Generally, automatic conflict resolution flags are best used when you’re very sure about the intended outcome.
Yeah I don’t trust automatic conflict resolution.
That’s reasonable. Manual resolution is slower but safer.
Another thing I struggle with: after a rebase, my branch diverges from remote and `git status` says I have to pull, but pulling would merge again. What’s the right move?

After rebasing a branch that has an upstream, your local branch history differs from the remote. You generally do not want to pull (which would merge or rebase again) at that point. Instead, you want to push your rewritten history: - `git push --force-with-lease` If you pull at that moment, you might create a merge commit that reintroduces the old history, defeating the purpose and making it messy. So after rebase of a pushed branch: 1) `git fetch` (optional but good to ensure you’re up to date) 2) `git push --force-with-lease`
What if the remote has new commits I don’t have?

Then `--force-with-lease` might refuse to push (good). You need to integrate those remote commits first. Options: - If those remote commits should be kept, you can rebase onto the updated remote branch: - `git fetch` - `git rebase origin/your-branch` Then push with force-with-lease. - Or merge them (but that may complicate). - Or if you intend to overwrite them (dangerous), you’d need to decide consciously—`--force-with-lease` helps prevent accidental overwrites.
So the lease is basically “only force if remote is what I think it is.”
Exactly.
Can you summarize “merge vs rebase” in one paragraph I can keep in my head?

Merge integrates two branches by creating a merge commit (or fast-forward) and preserves the original commits and branch structure; rebase moves a series of commits to a new base by recreating them with new parent links (new commit IDs), producing a linear history but rewriting those commits—so rebase is best for local/unpublished work you want to tidy, while merge is best when you want to preserve history and avoid rewriting commits that are already depended on.
That’s good. But I want a deeper intuition about why Git can’t just “change the parent” of a commit instead of creating new ones.

Because Git objects are immutable and content-addressed. The commit ID is the hash of the commit content, which includes the parent SHA(s). If you changed the parent, the content changes, so the hash changes. That means it’s a different object. This immutability is a feature: - Integrity: if you have a commit ID, you can be sure the content hasn’t been tampered with. - Deduplication: identical objects share storage. - Distribution: you can exchange objects safely knowing IDs correspond to content. So rewriting history necessarily creates new commits.
That makes sense. So “rewriting” is really “writing new commits and moving pointers.”
Exactly. Git never edits old commits; it creates new ones and updates refs.
Is there any way to “undo” a rebase if I already finished it?

Yes, typically via reflog. If you just rebased `feature`, you can find the pre-rebase tip: - `git reflog feature` Look for an entry like “rebase: checkout” or the previous HEAD. Then: - `git reset --hard <old-sha>` If you already force-pushed and want to restore the remote, you can push the old SHA back (again with force). But locally, reflog is the main undo mechanism.
What if garbage collection removed the old commits?

Usually reflog entries keep them reachable for a while (default 90 days for reflog, 30 days for unreachable objects, configurable). If a long time passes and GC prunes them, recovery becomes harder or impossible. But for typical “I just rebased and regret it” scenarios, reflog works.
Another thing: sometimes I rebase and my editor opens up with a list of commits. That’s interactive rebase. But sometimes it opens a commit message editor during a normal rebase. Why?

During a normal rebase, it may open an editor if: - A commit being replayed has a conflict and you had to resolve and continue, and Git wants you to edit the commit message (less common). - You used `--rebase-merges` or other options that require commit message edits. - There’s a commit that cannot be applied cleanly and you’re in an “edit” step. - Or you have config set to reword or to use interactive mode. Also, if a commit being applied is a merge commit (in certain modes), Git may need messages. But the most common editor pop-ups are: - interactive todo list (`rebase -i`) - commit message editing when you used `reword` or squashed commits (Git combines messages and opens an editor).
When squashing, I get a huge commit message with multiple messages. What’s the best practice there?

Typically: - Keep a single clear message describing the final change. - Optionally include relevant details from the squashed commits if they add value, but remove noise like “fix typo.” A good squashed commit message often includes: - What changed - Why - Any notable constraints/decisions - Maybe references to issues/specs if you use them (not required)
If I prefer not to squash, is it okay to leave small fix commits?

It’s okay, but it depends on how you want history to read. Small fix commits can be fine if they’re meaningful (“Fix edge case in parser”) rather than noise (“Oops”). If you care about a polished history, squashing fixups into the commit that introduced the bug is nicer. If you care about showing the real incremental process, leaving them is okay.
I think I’m getting it. But I want to practice: can you give me a scenario and ask me what I’d do—merge or rebase?

Scenario 1: You have a local branch `feature` with 5 commits not pushed anywhere. The base branch has advanced with new commits. You want to update your branch before continuing work and you want to avoid sync merge commits. What do you do?
I’d rebase `feature` onto the updated base, like `git fetch` then `git rebase origin/main`.

Exactly. Scenario 2: You have a branch that you’ve pushed and you’ve also created other local branches based on it. You realize you want to clean up commit messages and squash a couple of commits. What do you do?
Hmm. If I rebase, the other branches based on it will be affected. Maybe I’d avoid rebasing and just leave it, or create a new cleaned-up branch and cherry-pick?

That’s a good answer. If multiple downstream branches depend on it, rewriting it forces you to reconcile those branches too. Creating a new “clean” branch via cherry-pick or rebase onto base and then moving downstream branches can be done, but it’s more involved. Often, you either accept the history as-is or do the cleanup early before building more work on it. Scenario 3: You want to integrate a completed feature branch into main and you want the exact record of integration as a single point in history. What do you do?
Probably merge with `--no-ff` to create a merge commit.
Yes, that’s the typical choice for an explicit integration commit.
This is helping. I still have one lingering confusion: If I rebase and then later merge, do I risk duplicating commits?

If you rebase a feature branch onto main and then merge that rebased branch into main, you generally won’t duplicate commits—because the rebased commits are now descendants of main, so merging is either fast-forward or a simple merge. Duplication happens if: - You merge the original (pre-rebase) branch into main, and later also merge the rebased version (which contains the same changes but different commit IDs). Git can’t always recognize they’re the same changes, so you can end up with duplicated content or conflicts. So: don’t merge both the pre-rebase and post-rebase versions of the same work into the same target branch. After rebasing, treat the rebased branch as the “real” one and discard the old one (or keep it only as backup, not to be merged).
That’s a good warning. So if I’ve rebased, I shouldn’t keep the old remote branch around and merge it later.
Right. If the old branch still exists, make sure you don’t accidentally integrate it. A clear naming or deleting old refs helps.
Another confusion: what’s the difference between “rebase onto main” and “reset to main and cherry-pick my commits”? Aren’t they equivalent?

They’re very similar. - Rebase: automatically identifies the commits to replay and replays them in order. - Reset + cherry-pick: you manually move the branch pointer and pick commits. Example equivalence: - On `feature`: - `git rebase main` is similar to: - `git checkout feature` - `git reset --hard main` (dangerous; discards feature tip) - `git cherry-pick <commits that were on feature>` Rebase is safer and more convenient because it calculates the commit range and keeps a safety net via reflog. Reset is a blunt tool.
Yeah, reset scares me more.
That’s fair. Reset is very useful, but it’s easier to accidentally discard changes if you use `--hard` without understanding.
What about `git reset --soft` and `--mixed`? Do those relate here?

They’re not directly about merge vs rebase, but they’re useful in history editing. - `--soft`: moves HEAD to a commit but keeps index and working tree as-is (so changes are staged). - `--mixed` (default): moves HEAD and resets index to match, but keeps working tree changes (so changes become unstaged). - `--hard`: moves HEAD and resets index and working tree (discarding local changes). In interactive rebase when splitting commits, you often use `reset --mixed HEAD^` to uncommit but keep changes in working tree for restaging.
If I do rebase and I want to edit a commit, I mark it as `edit` and then do changes and `commit --amend`?
Exactly. Flow: - `git rebase -i <base>` - change `pick` to `edit` for a commit - when it stops: - modify files - `git add ...` - `git commit --amend` (to update that commit) - `git rebase --continue`
And that creates a new commit for the amended one, which is fine because we’re already rewriting.
Correct.
Last question for now: If I want to keep my history linear but I sometimes need to integrate changes from another branch into mine, should I always rebase?

If you want to keep your branch linear, prefer rebasing your branch onto the other branch rather than merging the other branch into yours. That keeps your history as a straight line. If you truly need to combine two independent lines of work and preserve that they were combined (i.e., you want a merge), then merging is appropriate. But if your goal is “my branch should be based on the latest base,” rebase is the tool.
Thanks. I think I finally understand the difference beyond the slogans.

Good. If you want, describe a specific repository state you’ve run into (what branches, what commits moved where, what command you ran), and I can tell you exactly what Git did and what the cleanest next step is—merge, rebase, reset, or cherry-pick.
My app has about 20 components that need shared state. Is Redux overkill?

It depends on what kind of shared state you have and how often it changes, but for a medium-sized app with ~20 components, Redux can be either “nice structure” or “unnecessary ceremony.” A good way to decide is to separate concerns: 1) Server/cache state (data from APIs) vs client/UI state (filters, selected items, open panels). 2) How many independent “domains” of state you have (auth/session, editor, cart, notifications, etc.). 3) Whether you need debugging/time travel, strict patterns, middleware, and predictable updates. If most of your shared state is UI-ish and localized to a few screens, React Context + hooks or Zustand often wins on simplicity. If you have complex cross-cutting logic, lots of async workflows, and you want strong conventions and tooling, Redux Toolkit (RTK) is typically the least painful Redux. Before picking a library, can you list the main shared state buckets you have (e.g., auth, settings, “current project,” etc.) and whether you’re also dealing with server data caching?

Shared state buckets: auth user + token, theme, feature flags, a “current workspace” selection, a list of notifications, and some UI state like sidebars and modals. Server data is fetched from a REST API; we don’t do caching beyond basic useEffect fetches right now.

That’s a helpful breakdown. A few observations: - Auth user/token: cross-cutting, needs persistence, and affects API requests. - Theme + feature flags: global-ish but relatively static. - Current workspace: global selection used in many API calls and screens. - Notifications: global list, often append/remove. - UI state (sidebars/modals): can be global if many screens interact with them, but often can be localized per layout. Given that you’re currently doing “basic useEffect fetches,” you might end up wanting a data-fetching/cache layer soon. If you adopt Redux Toolkit, RTK Query gives you caching, invalidation, and request deduping in a cohesive way. That’s one of the strongest reasons to pick Redux today. If you don’t want Redux, you can pair: - Zustand (or Context) for client state, and - React Query (TanStack Query) or SWR for server state caching. That combo is very common and ergonomic. Decision heuristic: - If you’re willing to introduce a dedicated server-state library anyway, Redux becomes less necessary unless you want its conventions/tooling. - If you want “one integrated stack” and can accept some ceremony, RTK + RTK Query is compelling. How complex are your async flows? For example: do you have multi-step actions like “switch workspace → refetch permissions → refetch user profile → reroute”? Or mostly straightforward fetch and display?
Mostly straightforward fetch and display. Switching workspace triggers refetch of a couple lists. Auth refresh token happens silently sometimes. Notifications come from polling.

With “straightforward fetch and display,” Redux isn’t required. You can keep things clean with either: A) Zustand for client state + React Query for server state B) Redux Toolkit + RTK Query for server state + slices for client state C) Pure Context + custom hooks + React Query Option A is usually the lowest-friction while staying scalable. Some tradeoffs: - React Context alone: fine for theme/feature flags/auth, but can lead to unnecessary rerenders if you cram lots into one provider. You can split contexts, but then you manage many providers and update patterns manually. - Zustand: minimal boilerplate, selective subscriptions avoid rerenders, easy to keep separate stores or one store. Good for UI state and app-level state. - Redux Toolkit: more structured; devtools story is excellent; middleware and patterns for larger teams; RTK Query can replace React Query in many cases. Given your list, I’d do: - React Query for REST fetching/caching - Zustand for auth + workspace + UI + notifications (or notifications in React Query if they’re server-derived) - Context for theme if you already use a UI library that expects it Do you need offline support, undo/redo, or complex derived state? Those can push you toward Redux (or at least a more formal store).
No offline. No undo/redo. Derived state is mostly things like “is user admin,” “current workspace id,” “has unread notifications.”

That’s well within Zustand/Context territory. One potential pitfall: auth token and API calls. If you use React Query, you’ll have a fetch client that needs access to the latest token. With Zustand, you can keep the token in the store and have your fetch wrapper read it at call time. With Context, similar. With Redux, you’d use middleware or baseQuery (RTK Query). If you go Zustand + React Query, a common pattern is: - A `useAuthStore` with `token`, `user`, `setToken`, `logout`. - An `apiFetch` function that reads `useAuthStore.getState().token` so it always gets the latest token without having to pass it around. - React Query uses `queryFn` that calls `apiFetch`. - On 401, refresh flow: either intercept in `apiFetch` or handle in query error boundary. For workspace switching: store `workspaceId` in Zustand, include it in React Query query keys, so switching workspace naturally refetches. Example conceptually: - Query key: `['projects', workspaceId]` - When `workspaceId` changes, queries with that key refetch. Does that align with how you’d like to structure it? Or do you want the store to “own” fetched lists too?
I’d rather not put fetched lists in the store if React Query can handle it. But I’m worried about “two sources of truth” and complexity.

That’s a reasonable concern, but the “two sources of truth” problem is mostly when you duplicate the same data in both places. If you draw a clear line: - React Query: server state (anything fetched, cached, synced with backend) - Zustand/Context: client state (UI toggles, selections, auth/session, workspace selection) Then you avoid duplication. Complexity actually tends to decrease because: - You stop writing manual loading/error caching logic. - You stop writing “store slices” for server lists. - React Query gives you consistent patterns for invalidation and refetch. The key is discipline: don’t copy server responses into Zustand unless you have a strong reason (like editing a draft locally). Even then, store the draft separately, not the canonical server list. If you want an even simpler mental model, Redux Toolkit + RTK Query also draws a similar line, but within Redux. It’s not inherently “one source of truth” either—RTK Query has its own cache slice managed for you. What specifically worries you about complexity: debugging, mental model, boilerplate, or performance?
Mainly debugging and mental model. If something shows stale data, I want to know where it came from. Also performance: I don’t want rerenders everywhere.

For debugging stale data: - React Query has devtools that show query keys, freshness, cache times, last updated, observers, etc. It’s very good for answering “why is this stale?” - Zustand has simple devtools integration too, but even without it, the state is small and explicit. Mental model: - “If it comes from the server: React Query; if it’s UI/session: Zustand.” - “Server data is keyed by parameters (workspaceId, filters). Changing params changes the query key.” Performance: - React Query updates only components that use the query. - Zustand can subscribe to slices so only components that select relevant parts rerender. With Context, performance can be trickier if you put too much in one provider; every consumer rerenders on any change. You can mitigate with multiple contexts or context selector libraries, but that’s extra complexity. So from your concerns, Zustand + React Query is a strong fit. If you want something even more “single tool,” Redux Toolkit also performs well and has Redux DevTools which are excellent for tracing actions. But you’ll still likely use RTK Query and the mental model becomes “actions update store; queries update cache slice.” How many developers are working on this app? Team size affects how valuable strict patterns are.
Let’s assume a small team, like 3-4 devs. They’re comfortable with React but not all are Redux experts.

With 3–4 devs and no strong Redux background, Redux Toolkit is still approachable, but it’s a bigger conceptual jump than Zustand. Zustand is closer to “hooks with a global store,” and tends to have fewer “rules.” For maintainability with a small team: - Zustand store modules per domain (auth, ui, workspace) - Consistent patterns for actions (setters) and selectors - React Query for data That gives you enough structure without heavy ceremony. If you do consider Redux, I’d strongly recommend only Redux Toolkit (not “classic Redux”). Classic Redux adds a lot of boilerplate and footguns. But you can stay pragmatic: start with Zustand + React Query; if later you truly need Redux-level tooling, it’s possible to migrate, though not free. Do you have any constraints like SSR (Next.js) or React Native? Those can influence setup.
It’s a standard SPA, Vite + React. No SSR. Typescript.

Great; that simplifies things. With TypeScript, both Zustand and Redux Toolkit have good typing stories: - Zustand: types are straightforward; you define the store shape and actions. - Redux Toolkit: also good; slices infer action types; RTK Query can infer types from endpoints. If you choose Zustand, consider: - Use `zustand/middleware` like `devtools`, `persist` for auth token/workspace selection. - Keep store small and avoid storing derived values; compute derived values with selectors or helper functions. Example store shape (conceptual): - auth: `{ user, token, status }` - workspace: `{ currentWorkspaceId }` - ui: `{ sidebarOpen, modal: { type, props } }` - notifications: `{ items, unreadCount }` or compute unread count React Query: - Set default `staleTime` and `cacheTime` appropriately. - Use `queryKey` conventions with workspaceId. - Use mutations and `invalidateQueries` after updates. Are your feature flags coming from server or build-time config?
From server at login, but they rarely change. We might refresh them occasionally.

That’s a good candidate for React Query too, but you could also load them once and store in Zustand. The difference: - If feature flags are purely server-derived and you want caching/refetch semantics, React Query fits. - If feature flags are essentially “session config” and used widely, storing in Zustand after initial fetch is fine. A hybrid approach: - Fetch flags with React Query on login/session initialization. - In the `onSuccess`, store them in Zustand if you want easy access without hooks everywhere.
